headline: Paralyzed Man Feeds Himself Again Through Groundbreaking Brain Surgery
meta_description: Keith Thomas regained arm and hand control after double neural bypass surgery connecting his brain to his limbs, even feeling his dog's fur.
summary_short: A man paralyzed from a diving accident now feeds himself and pets his dog thanks to surgery that routes brain signals directly to his arms.
body:
Keith Thomas from Massapequa broke his neck diving into a pool in summer 2020, becoming paralyzed from the chest down. Three months later, he was offered enrollment in a groundbreaking trial at the Feinstein Institutes for Medical Research. The double neural bypass surgery implants electrodes in his brain to detect when he wants to move his arms, then routes those signals directly to his limbs—bypassing the damaged spinal cord entirely. A second set of sensors in his fingers sends touch information back to his brain, allowing him to feel textures, detect objects, and adjust his grip strength. The results have been striking: after 35 weeks of training, Thomas gained 86 percent strength in his right arm and 62 percent in his left. He can now scratch his nose, wipe his mouth, and feel the fur of his dog—movements he couldn't perform before, when his hands remained locked below his neck.

What makes this breakthrough particularly remarkable is that Thomas's gains persist even when the computer system is turned off, suggesting genuine neurological recovery rather than mere technological compensation. The technology doesn't just restore movement; it restores the sensation of touch—allowing him to distinguish between holding a coffee cup, an egg, or a ball, and to adjust pressure accordingly. These aren't abstract gains. They're the difference between dependence and dignity, between isolation and connection.

Professor Chad Bouton, who led the research team, emphasizes that the full extent of Thomas's recovery remains unknown. With millions worldwide living with paralysis, this technology represents a new frontier in restoring not just function, but the embodied experience of being in the world. The work was recently published in Nature, offering hope to countless others awaiting similar breakthroughs.
quick_facts: Keith Thomas from Massapequa, New York, broke his neck in a diving accident
Double neural bypass surgery bypasses damaged spinal cord using brain implants
After 35 weeks of training, he gained 86% strength in right arm, 62% in left
Technology allows him to feel textures and adjust grip pressure independently
Gains persist even when the computer system is turned off
